exercises.title: Runtime dispatch
hints: Reference type is A — compile check uses A
Object type is B — runtime dispatch uses B
B overrides greet() → B's version runs

exercises.title: Mixed array iteration
exercises.scenario: Different objects in same array, polymorphic calls.
exercises.expected_solution:
Drawing circle
Drawing shape
Drawing square

test_cases.expected:
Drawing circle
Drawing shape
Drawing square

hints: Circle overrides draw → 'Drawing circle'
Shape (plain) → 'Drawing shape'
Square overrides draw → 'Drawing square'

exercises.title: Write a polymorphic method
exercises.scenario: Method accepting parent type, works for all subclasses.
exercises.instructions:
Given Animal (makeSound), Dog (Woof), Cat (Meow): Write a static method `announceAnimal(Animal a)` that prints 'Animal announcement: ' then calls a.makeSound().

Call it with a Dog and a Cat.

Expected output:
Animal announcement: Woof!
Animal announcement: Meow!

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Animal announcement: Woof!
Animal announcement: Meow!

hints: Method signature: static void announceAnimal(Animal a)
Inside: print the prefix, then call a.makeSound()
Runtime dispatch picks Dog's or Cat's makeSound

exercises.title: Fix the compile error — calling subclass method on parent ref
exercises.scenario: Can't call subclass-specific method on parent reference.
exercises.instructions:
This code doesn't compile. Fix it using instanceof and downcast.

Expected output:
Rex fetches!

bugs.issue: fetch() not defined in Animal — can't call on Animal reference

hints: Animal reference can't access Dog-specific methods
Use: if (a instanceof Dog d) { d.fetch(); }
This checks type AND creates Dog variable `d`

exercises.title: Safe downcasting
exercises.instructions:
Create Animal, Dog, Cat. Write method `doSpecificThing(Animal a)` that:
- If Dog: calls fetch()
- If Cat: calls purr()
- Otherwise: prints 'Unknown animal'

Test with Dog, Cat, and a plain Animal.

Expected output:
Rex fetches!
Whiskers purrs!
Unknown animal

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Rex fetches!
Whiskers purrs!
Unknown animal

hints: Pattern: if (a instanceof Dog d) { d.fetch(); }
chain with else if for Cat
else handles plain Animal or unknown types
null instanceof X is false — safe

exercises.title: totalArea method
exercises.instructions:
Create Shape (abstract area() — just return 0 for now), Circle (area = πr²), Rectangle (area = w×h).

Write `totalArea(Shape[] shapes)` that sums all areas. Demonstrate with mixed array.

Expected output:
Total area: 113.09733552923255
exercises.starter_code:
// Create Shape, Circle, Rectangle
// Write totalArea(Shape[] shapes) using polymorphism
hints: totalArea accepts Shape[] — works for any subclass
Each s.area() calls the right override

exercises.title: Polymorphic salary calculator
exercises.scenario: Callback to Topic 5.1 exercise — formalize the polymorphism.
exercises.instructions:
Using Employee (baseSalary, calculateSalary() = base), Manager (+ bonus), Contractor (hourlyRate + hoursWorked):

Write `printPayroll(Employee[] staff)` that prints each name and calculated salary.

Create a mixed array with 3 employees. Print payroll.

Expected output:
Aisha: ₹75000.0
Raj: ₹100000.0
Priya: ₹57500.0
exercises.starter_code:
// Employee, Manager, Contractor classes + printPayroll
// Each has different calculateSalary() override
hints: printPayroll takes Employee[] — polymorphic
e.calculateSalary() dispatches to each type's override
Contractor: 2500 × 23 = 57500

exercises.title: Renderer system
exercises.scenario: Polymorphic rendering — adding new types never changes the renderer.
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- `Renderable` class: renderMode (String). render() prints '[renderMode]: <specific info>'
- `TextLabel extends Renderable`: text (String). Override render: '[TEXT]: text'
- `ImageWidget extends Renderable`: imagePath (String), width, height. Override render: '[IMAGE]: path (w×h)'
- `ButtonWidget extends Renderable`: label (String). Override render: '[BUTTON]: label'

Write `renderAll(Renderable[] components)` that calls render on each.

Expected output:
[TEXT]: Hello World
[IMAGE]: logo.png (200×100)
[BUTTON]: Submit
exercises.starter_code:
// Create Renderable, TextLabel, ImageWidget, ButtonWidget
// renderAll uses polymorphism
hints: renderAll accepts Renderable[] — polymorphic
Each r.render() dispatches to the specific type
This is literally how UI toolkits (Swing, JavaFX) work

exercises.title: Fix unsafe downcast
exercises.instructions:
This code crashes at runtime with ClassCastException. Fix it.

Expected output:
Rex fetches!
Whiskers: not a dog

bugs.issue: Unchecked downcast — crashes if a is not a Dog

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Rex fetches!
Whiskers: not a dog

hints: ClassCastException: can't cast Cat to Dog at runtime
Fix: if (a instanceof Dog d) { d.fetch(); }
else: handle the non-Dog case

exercises.title: Polymorphic tax calculator
exercises.scenario: Different income sources taxed differently via polymorphism.
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- `Income`: amount (double). `taxDue()` returns 0 (no default tax).
- `SalaryIncome extends Income`: override taxDue() → amount × 0.20 (20% flat)
- `FreelanceIncome extends Income`: override taxDue() → amount × 0.15 (15%)
- `RentalIncome extends Income`: override taxDue() → amount × 0.10 (10%)

Write `totalTax(Income[] sources)` summing taxDue for all.

Expected output:
Total tax: ₹21000.0
hints: Each income type overrides taxDue() with its rate
totalTax uses polymorphism: inc.taxDue() for each

exercises.title: Sort animals by sound length (instanceof for specifics)
exercises.scenario: Processing that needs both polymorphic and type-specific logic.
exercises.instructions:
Given Animal (makeSound returns String), Dog ('Woof'), Cat ('Meow'), Wolf ('Awooo'):

Write `findLoudest(Animal[] animals)` that returns the Animal whose sound string is LONGEST.

Expected output:
Loudest: Awooo
hints: makeSound() returns String — use .length() to compare
Start with animals[0] as loudest
Compare via makeSound().length() — polymorphic dispatch handles the right sound
Return the Animal object (not the string)

exercises.title: Tracing reference type vs object type
hints: b.info(): Child overrides info() → 'Child' runs
b.fixed(): Child doesn't override → Base's version runs
Reference type determines WHAT you can call, object type determines WHICH implementation

exercises.title: Logger strategy pattern
exercises.instructions:
Create a logging system:
- `Logger` base: logLevel (String). `log(String msg)` prints '[logLevel] msg'.
- `ConsoleLogger extends Logger`: override log() prints to console with prefix '[CONSOLE][logLevel]'
- `FileLogger extends Logger`: filename (String). Override log(): prints '[FILE: filename][logLevel] msg'
- `NullLogger extends Logger`: override log() — does NOTHING (silences logging)

Write `logAll(Logger logger, String[] messages)` that logs all messages.

In main, use same messages with ConsoleLogger, then NullLogger.

Expected output:
[CONSOLE][INFO] Starting app
[CONSOLE][INFO] User logged in
(NullLogger prints nothing)
exercises.starter_code:
// Logger, ConsoleLogger, FileLogger, NullLogger
// logAll demonstrates polymorphism
hints: logAll accepts Logger — works for any logger type
NullLogger.log() does nothing — no output
This is the 'Null Object' and 'Strategy' patterns combined

exercises.title: Discount system — extensible via polymorphism
exercises.scenario: Open/Closed Principle: add discounts without changing checkout.
exercises.instructions:
Create:
- `Discount`: basePrice (double). `apply(double price)` returns price (no discount).
- `PercentageDiscount extends Discount`: percent (double). apply: price × (1 - percent/100)
- `FlatDiscount extends Discount`: amount (double). apply: max(0, price - amount)
- `BuyOneGetOneFree extends Discount`: apply: price / 2 (50% off)

Write `checkout(double price, Discount[] discounts)` — applies each discount sequentially and prints final.

Test with price ₹1000, applying: 10% off, then ₹50 flat off.

Expected output:
Final price: ₹850.0
exercises.starter_code:
// Discount, PercentageDiscount, FlatDiscount, BuyOneGetOneFree
// checkout applies discounts sequentially
hints: checkout loops through discounts, applying each in turn
price = d.apply(price) chains the discounts
Adding new discount types: just extend Discount, no changes to checkout

exercises.title: Event processing pipeline
exercises.scenario: Polymorphic event handlers — real framework design.
exercises.instructions:
Build an event system:
1. `Event` base: String type, long timestamp (use System.currentTimeMillis()). toString.
2. `LoginEvent extends Event`: username. Override toString to include username.
3. `PurchaseEvent extends Event`: amount, productName. Override toString.
4. `EventHandler` base: `handle(Event e)` prints 'Handling event: ' + e.
5. `AuditHandler extends EventHandler`: override handle to print 'AUDIT: ' + e
6. `AlertHandler extends EventHandler`: override — ONLY prints for PurchaseEvent > ₹10000 (use instanceof).

Create pipeline with both handlers. Process 3 events.

Expected output:
AUDIT: [LOGIN] aisha (at <timestamp>)
AUDIT: [PURCHASE] Laptop ₹75000 (at <timestamp>)
AUDIT: [PURCHASE] Coffee ₹150 (at <timestamp>)
ALERT: Large purchase: [PURCHASE] Laptop ₹75000 (at <timestamp>)
exercises.starter_code:
// Event hierarchy + EventHandler hierarchy
// AlertHandler uses instanceof to filter
hints: AuditHandler prints ALL events
AlertHandler only prints PurchaseEvent with amount > 10000
AlertHandler uses instanceof PurchaseEvent pe to access getAmount()
Outer loop: each handler. Inner loop: each event.
This is exactly how Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher works
